problem_summary: 2020 MCM/ICM Problem B (discrete) — "Sandcastle foundation" optimization. The team had to (1) find the most stable 3D geometric shape for a sandcastle foundation eroded by waves/tides, (2) find the optimal sand-to-water mixture proportion, and (3) re-evaluate the optimal shape under rainfall. This paper (#2007698) reframes the whole problem as a 3D periodic cellular automaton ("sand-water cell automaton") in which the foundation is discretized into stacked rigid sand cells, water cells, and empty cells, and erosion emerges from local state-transition rules. They test 5 candidate frusta (triangular, square, six-arris/hexagonal, conical, ellipse) by simulating wave attack and measuring "lasting time" = number of timesteps until the top surface cell count G_i drops below half of its initial value G_0. Conclusions: triangular frustum is most durable under waves; an optimal water-sand ratio (reported inconsistently as ~0.05 / 0.55); and under rainfall both triangular and ellipse frustum perform best.
models_used: 3D periodic cellular automaton (the core model) — 3-state cells (empty / water / sand) on a 3*width x 2*long x 4*height lattice with 26-neighbor (Moore) neighborhoods and local transition rules

Discrete erosion/avalanche dynamics encoded as an 'instable factor' rule: K = F - sigma * sum_j(U_j), where F = adjacent water cells and U_j = surrounding sand-cell counts; a sand cell 'falls' (moves down/horizontally toward most water) when F>=P and K>=P
Capillarity / cohesion proxy rule: a water cell surrounded by >=15 sand cells is locked in place (mimics capillary water retention inside the foundation)
Tidal wave model: piecewise sinusoidal wave height h = 2H*sin(2*pi*i/d) on alternating tidal half-periods, injected as water cells on the left boundary

Sediment-transport physics for parameterization: Dou Guoren's sediment-carrying equation d(hs)/dt + d(hvs)/dx + alpha*omega(S - S*) = 0, simplified at steady state so transport capacity = sediment content; used to derive the fall threshold P from wave sediment-carrying volume S_v via P/27 >= 1 - S_v

Least-squares polynomial curve fitting (10-degree polynomial) of (water:sand ratio, lasting-time) data points to locate the optimal mixture proportion — 'concentration gradient method' for sampling the ratio

Rainfall module (modified CA): top-layer cells spontaneously become water cells with probability u = H/(20d) each step, superimposed on the wave module; bottom infiltration/percolation with probability v = H/(2d)
Local sensitivity analysis (one-at-a-time +/-5%, +/-10% perturbation) on S_v, base height H, base width d, stability coefficient sigma, and collapse threshold G_min
key_techniques: Reframing a vague physical-stability question as a discrete agent-based simulation rather than a closed-form continuum/slope-stability PDE — sidesteps the intractable 3D limit-equilibrium analysis the literature review flags as limited
Defining a single crisp, measurable output metric ('lasting time' = timesteps until G_i < G_0/2) so heterogeneous shapes become directly comparable and rankable

Grounding free parameters in real physics/literature instead of guessing: P from Dou's sediment equation + a cited 55% sediment-carrying volume; optimal-ratio sanity-checked against a cited clay/sand ratio range of 0.43-0.66 (Emiroglu 2015)
Encoding cohesion and capillarity as simple integer neighbor-count thresholds (15-neighbor water lock; sigma-weighted instability factor K) — physically motivated yet cheap to compute on a 3D lattice

Modular model design: Task 2 reuses the Task 1 CA verbatim (only sweeping the ratio); Task 3 just bolts a rainfall module onto the same engine — strong reuse and a clean similarities/differences narrative

MATLAB implementation provided in full (createThreePyramid/Four/Six/Circular geometry builders via symbolic plane equations, moveSand, moveWater, permeate, createWave, createRain, getUnstableFactor) — analytic-geometry plane-cutting to voxelize each frustum

Physical-intuition storytelling to explain the counterintuitive winner: triangular front acts like a ship's bow / herringbone geese formation to shed wave force, making the discrete result believable to judges
Per-shape 3D scatter visualizations of begin/process/end states plus a fitting curve, giving visible evidence for each claim
why_it_won: "From a judge's view it wins on a creative, well-matched modeling choice executed end-to-end. (1) The CA framing is genuinely original and a perfect fit for a discrete-type problem about an inherently granular, local-interaction medium — it turns an analytically nasty 3D slope-stability problem into something simulable and visual. (2) It actually answers all three tasks with concrete deliverables (triangular frustum; an optimal ratio; rainfall-modified shape ranking), not just one. (3) Parameters are tied to cited sediment-transport and granular-physics literature, signaling rigor rather than hand-waving. (4) Excellent engineering hygiene: one reusable simulation engine across all three tasks, full reproducible MATLAB code, sensitivity analysis, and an explicit strengths/weaknesses section. (5) Strong communication — a counterintuitive result (triangle beats the 'obvious' round castle) is defended with vivid fluid-dynamics analogies, and the required 'Fun in the Sun' popular article is delivered in plain, engaging language. The combination of novelty + completeness + reproducibility + readable narrative is the O-award signature."
section_highlights: "Structure follows the canonical O-paper skeleton and uses it well. Summary is benefit-led and states all three numeric answers up front. Literature Review (1.2) is unusually strong: it organizes prior work into four themed buckets (sandpile, sediment models, slope stability, sandcastle physics) and explicitly positions the CA approach as a new angle on slope stability — this framing is a big part of why the model reads as principled. Section 2 separates global assumptions, notations table, and per-section local assumptions cleanly. Model sections 3-5 each follow Principle -> Assumptions -> Construction -> Rules -> Algorithm steps -> Result -> Analysis, with mermaid flowcharts for every algorithm. The 'Analysis of Result' subsections add interpretation (ship-bow / herringbone-geese analogy) rather than just dumping plots. Sensitivity analysis is tabulated (Table 3) with +/-5/10% perturbations and timed outcomes. Dedicated Strategies section translates findings into actionable beach advice, and the required popular-science 'The Secret of Sandcastle Building' article is well-written for a lay audience. Full commented MATLAB appendix makes it reproducible."
pitfalls_or_limitations: Internal numerical inconsistency on the headline answers: the Summary says optimal water-sand ratio = 0.55, but Section 4 fits the data and concludes 'around 0.05' (and the popular article repeats 0.05). These cannot both be right and a careful judge would notice.

S_v (wave sediment-carrying volume) is set to 25% in Section 3.1.6 (giving P=12) but to 55% in the assumptions and sensitivity section — the key parameter is used at two different values without reconciliation.

Table 2 is mislabeled: the header says 'water:sand' but the column is the same quantity called the 'sand-to-water' ratio elsewhere; combined with M=3 'temporarily' and m_j = j/2 sampling up to 40, the ratio bookkeeping is muddled and hard to reproduce exactly.

Only a 9-point dataset is fit with a 10-degree polynomial — this is over-fitting (a degree-10 polynomial through ~9 points), so the 'optimal' extracted from the curve is not statistically trustworthy.
Sensitivity analysis was run only on the triangular prism 'due to time constraints', so robustness of the shape ranking itself (the central claim) is not tested.

Many transition-rule constants (the 15-neighbor water lock, sigma, infiltration probabilities H/(2d), rain probability H/(20d)) are asserted with little justification or units; results may be artifacts of these tunings.
Wave-only surface erosion is modeled; structural/tidal undermining, evaporation, and large waves are explicitly excluded, limiting real-world applicability (acknowledged in Weaknesses).
The extracted-image data tables in the markdown are OCR noise and not real data; the substantive evidence is the scatter snapshots and the fitting curve, which are qualitative.
reusable_patterns: When a problem involves a granular/local-interaction medium or an analytically intractable continuum (slope stability, crowd flow, fire/erosion spread, traffic), reframe it as a cellular automaton / agent-based simulation with simple local transition rules — discrete-type ICM problems reward this.
Define ONE crisp scalar output metric (here: timesteps until a surface-cell count halves) so that heterogeneous candidate designs become directly comparable and you can produce a clean ranking.

Build a single reusable simulation engine, then answer later tasks by (a) sweeping a parameter over it (ratio study) and (b) bolting on an additive module (rainfall) — explicitly write a 'Similarities and Differences from the basic model' subsection to make the reuse legible.

Derive free thresholds from cited domain physics (sediment-transport equation -> fall threshold P) instead of guessing, and sanity-check optimization outputs against a cited empirical range — this is what makes a simulation read as rigorous rather than arbitrary.

Pair every algorithm with a mermaid/flowchart and every result claim with a before/in-process/after visualization plus a short physical-intuition 'why' (analogy) — judges trust results they can both see and understand.

Ship the full, sectioned source code in an appendix using analytic-geometry plane equations to voxelize arbitrary shapes — makes the work reproducible and extensible ('change a few parameters to test any shape').

Include an explicit Strengths/Weaknesses/Future-work section and a separate actionable-recommendations section that translates math back to the stakeholder's language (and, when required, a genuinely lay-readable popular article).

Caution to copy the rigor but not the flaws: keep numeric answers consistent across summary/body/article, justify every magic constant with units, and do not fit a high-degree polynomial to a tiny dataset — use a low-order/robust fit and run sensitivity on the headline claim, not just one case.
